mnemonics.title: RSA Key Generation Steps
mnemonics.content: PICK (Prime selection) → N Equals p×q → φ = (p-1)(q-1) → E (choose public exponent) → D = e⁻¹ mod φ(n)

mnemonics.title: Digital Signature Process
mnemonics.content: HASH (Compute message digest) → SIGN (Encrypt hash with private key) → SEND (Send signature + message)

mnemonics.title: Signature Verification
mnemonics.content: RECEIVE (Get message + signature) → HASH (Hash received message) → VERIFY (Decrypt signature with public key, compare hashes)

acronyms.usage: Framework for managing digital certificates and public-key encryption
Trusted entity that issues and signs digital certificates
Entity that verifies identity before certificate issuance by CA
List of certificates that have been revoked before expiration
Asymmetric cryptography using elliptic curve mathematics

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a mailbox with two keys: a public key (open slot - anyone can drop messages) and a private key (the only key that can open and retrieve messages)
Think of it like a wax seal on a letter - the sender's private key 'melts' the unique seal, and recipients use the sender's public key to 'verify' the seal authenticity
Two large prime numbers (p and q) multiply to form n, like mixing two paint colors - easy to mix but nearly impossible to separate back into original colors
Imagine a hierarchy of notaries: Root CA is like the government, intermediate CAs are like licensed notaries, end certificates are like notarized documents

analogies.analogy: Like a mailbox with a public slot (anyone can drop letters) but only the owner has the key to open and read them
Like a handwritten signature but mathematically unique - like a fingerprint that only the signer possesses
Like a passport office - the government (root CA) verifies your identity and issues a document (certificate) that others trust
Like a web of notaries where you trust someone who vouches for others - establishing a chain of trust
